text: The surgeon performs dissection of the mesopancreas, focusing on the uncinate process and its separation from retroperitoneal structures, the superior mesenteric artery, and vein. The superior mesenteric vein is dissected, with attention to the origin of the first regional vein indicated by a yellow arrow. The portal vein is dissected laterally, with traction applied to the duodenum in the supracolic region. The assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein and mesentery to the left, while the surgeon retracts the duodenum to the right, completing the cattle brush maneuver to fully expose the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the first jejunal vein, marked by a yellow arrow, as it inserts into the superior mesenteric vein. The assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein to the left, exposing the first jejunal branch, while the surgeon retracts the duodenum and jejunal loop in the supracolic compartment to the left. Dissection occurs above the superior mesenteric artery, with the first jejunal vein clearly visible.

The surgeon performs suction dissection over the superior mesenteric artery, revealing the first jejunal vein as it reinserts into the superior mesenteric vein. The pulsations of the superior mesenteric artery are visible behind the first jejunal vein. The vein is seen kinking and rotating above and then descending behind the artery, hooking around it. Tributaries of the first jejunal vein are ligated and dissected, while the main vein is preserved. The dissection continues above the superior mesenteric artery, ensuring complete removal of the mesopancreas.

The surgeon retracts the specimen to the left side of the screen while the assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein or portal vein to the right, exposing the mesopancreas or pancreatic mesentery over the superior mesenteric artery. The first jejunal vein branch is identified, and its tributaries are clipped. The first regional branch is seen hooking around the superior mesenteric artery, ensuring complete removal of the mesopancreas.

The surgeon has reached the retroperitoneum, with the pancreatic parenchyma dissected and included in the specimen. The superior mesenteric artery is visible to the right of the superior mesenteric vein due to opposing tractions by the surgeon and assistant. The harmonic device is actively used on the superior mesenteric artery, which is identified behind the first jejunal vein. The dissection progresses along the superior mesenteric artery, encountering the superior pancreaticoduodenal vein entering the superior mesenteric vein, facilitating complete mesopancreas resection.
